---
abstract: "This chapter provides practical guidance on selecting evaluation measures in survival analysis, emphasizing alignment between the chosen metric and the goal of the analysis. Discrimination measures are recommended for assessing ranking performance, calibration methods for evaluating agreement between predicted and observed outcomes, and scoring rules for capturing overall predictive accuracy. For discrimination, Harrell's C and Uno's C are highlighted as robust default choices, with the latter preferred under heavier censoring. Calibration is best assessed visually using comparisons to the Kaplan-Meier estimator for single models, while D-calibration is recommended for comparing models. For overall performance, the integrated survival Brier score and the right-censored log loss are proposed, with results reported using explained residual variation to aid interpretation. The chapter also discusses measure selection for model tuning, recommending scoring rules for general use and Uno's C for ranking-focused tasks, while noting ongoing uncertainty in evaluating survival time predictions."

---

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
{{< include _macros.tex >}}
:::

# Choosing Measures {#sec-choosing-measures}

There are many survival measures\index{survival measures} to choose from, and selecting the right one for a given task may seem daunting.
Below are a few heuristics to support decision-making.
First and foremost, evaluation should always be according to the goals of analysis, which means using discrimination measures\index{discrimination} to evaluate relative risk predictions\index{relative risks}, calibration\index{calibration} measures to evaluate average performance, and scoring rules\index{scoring rules} to evaluate overall performance and distribution predictions.

For discrimination measures, consider Harrell's C and Uno's C.\index{concordance index!Harrell's C}\index{concordance index!Uno's C}
While others can assess time-dependent trends, these are also captured in scoring rules.
If only one measure is to be reported then Uno's C may be preferred.
Although it has higher variance compared to other concordance measures [@Rahman2017; @Schmid2012], it is less likely to overestimate concordance compared to Harrell's C as censoring increases [@Rahman2017]. Moreover, the variance can be transparently captured and reported through techniques such as bootstrapping.
In practice, the choice of measure matters less than ensuring your reporting is transparent and honest [@Therneau2020; @Sonabend2021].\index{C-hacking}

To assess the calibration of a single model, graphical comparisons to the Kaplan-Meier estimator\index{Kaplan-Meier estimator} provide a useful and interpretable method to quickly see if a model is a good fit to the data (@sec-calib-km). \index{calibration!plots}
When choosing between models, D-calibration\index{calibration!d-calibration} can be meaningfully optimized and thus used for model comparison.
<!-- TODO (RAPHAEL): add a caveat here. Optimizing for calibration alone can de-emphasize discrimination --- in the extreme, it pushes toward a featureless Kaplan-Meier model, which is perfectly calibrated but has no discriminative ability. This mirrors optimizing for discrimination while ignoring calibration, and should be stated explicitly so neither aspect is optimized in isolation. -->


When picking scoring rules, the RCLL\index{right-censored log loss} (@sec-rules-log) is proven strictly proper [@Rindt2022; @Yanagisawa2023] and intuitive, but may require numerical approximation of the density function (@sec-distributions-continuous).
Alternatively, use the survival Brier score evaluated at a specific time point of interest (@sec-pecs), or the ISBS (@sec-rules-squared).
<!-- TODO (RAPHAEL): can we define the SBS (survival Brier score at a single time point) separately? I think it has been proven proper (at least in our scoring rule paper) and could be used when a specific time point is of interest. At least the term "SBS" should appear somewhere (e.g. in @sec-pecs) so the back-reference here is clear. -->

Importantly, the choice of evaluation measure(s) should be decided up-front and not selected based on (preliminary) results.
When reporting scoring rules, consider the ERV representation which provides a meaningful interpretation as "performance increase over baseline".\index{explained residual variation}

Given the limited research currently available, evaluation of survival time predictions should be treated with caution and new developments in the literature should be monitored.

<!-- FIXME (RAPHAEL): the abstract contradicts this paragraph. The abstract's tuning clause says "Uno's C for ranking-focused tasks", but here (and correctly) we recommend Harrell's C for ranking-focused/separation tuning. Uno's C is the *evaluation* pick (unbiased single number); Harrell's C is the *tuning* pick (shared bias largely cancels in relative comparison, lower variance). Please update the abstract to say "Harrell's C for ranking-focused tasks". -->
For automated model optimization\index{tuning}, tuning with a scoring rule should capture discrimination and calibration simultaneously [@Kopper2026; @Rindt2022; @Yanagisawa2023].
However, if you are developing a model to maximize separation and calibration truly matters less, then prefer Harrell's C over Uno's C, as any bias in the measure should affect models similarly, making lower variance potentially preferable.
